question_text: Choose the correct option of temperature at which carbon reduces FeO to iron and produces CO.
question_html: Choose the correct option of temperature at which carbon reduces FeO to iron and produces CO.

options.text: Below temperature at point A.
options.html: Below temperature at point A.

options.text: Approximately at the temperature corresponding to point A.
options.html: Approximately at the temperature corresponding to point A.

options.text: Above temperature at point A but below temperature at point D.
options.html: Above temperature at point A but below temperature at point D.

options.text: Above temperature at point A.
options.html: Above temperature at point A.
options.explanation: FeO + C {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe + CO It can be seen as a couple of two simpler reactions: FeO {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe + {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} O 2 ( {tex}\triangle{/tex} G° FeO.Fe ) C + {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} O 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO ( {tex}\triangle{/tex} G° C.CO ) Total Gibb's energy change becomes {tex}\triangle{/tex} G° (C.CO) + ( {tex}\triangle{/tex} G° (FeO.Fe) {tex}\triangle{/tex} r G° In {tex}\triangle{/tex} G° versus T plot, the plot Fe to FeO goes upward direction and the plot for C to CO goes toward downwards direction. At temperature above point A, the C to CO line comes below Fe to FeO and {tex}\triangle{/tex} G° (C.CO) < {tex}\triangle{/tex} G° (Fe.FeO) . So in this range, C will reduce FeO to Fe and itself be oxidized to CO.

question_text: Which of the following options is correct? Cast iron is obtained by remelting pig iron with scrap iron and coke using hot air blast. In the extraction of silver, silver is extracted as a cationic complex. Nickel is purified by zone refining. Zr and Ti are purified by Van Arkel method.
question_html:
Which of the following options is correct?

  1. Cast iron is obtained by remelting pig iron with scrap iron and coke using hot air blast.
  2. In the extraction of silver, silver is extracted as a cationic complex.
  3. Nickel is purified by zone refining.
  4. Zr and Ti are purified by Van Arkel method.
solution_text: (a, d). Iron obtained from a blast furnace is called pig iron. It contains about 4% carbon and many impurities in small amounts such as Mn, P, Si, etc. The pig iron is remelted with scrap iron. This is known as cast iron. It has slightly less carbon content (3%) and is extremely hard and brittle. van Arkel method used for refining of Zr andTi {tex}\mathop {{\text{Zr(s)}}}\limits_{{\text{Impure}}} \,{\text{2}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{870}}\,{\text{K}}}}{\text{Zr}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{(g)}}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{{\text{tungsten}}\,{\text{filament}}}]{{{\text{2075K}}}}\mathop {{\text{Zr(s)}}}\limits_{{\text{Pure}}} {\text{ + 2}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex} Ti + 2l 2 {tex}\xrightarrow{{{\text{530K}}}}{/tex} Ti 4 {tex}\xrightarrow{{{\text{1700K}}}}{/tex} Ti + 2l 2
solution_html:
(a, d). Iron obtained from a blast furnace is called pig iron. It contains about 4% carbon and many impurities in small amounts such as Mn, P, Si, etc. The pig iron is remelted with scrap iron. This is known as cast iron. It has slightly less carbon content (3%) and is extremely hard and brittle.

question_text: Which of the following statements is correct about the role of substances added in the froth flotation process? Collectors enhance the non-wettability of the mineral particles. Collectors enhance the wettability of gangue particles. By using depressants in the process two sulphide ores can be separated. Froth stabilisers decrease the wettability of gangue.
question_html:
Which of the following statements is correct about the role of substances added in the froth flotation process?

  1. Collectors enhance the non-wettability of the mineral particles.
  2. Collectors enhance the wettability of gangue particles.
  3. By using depressants in the process two sulphide ores can be separated.
  4. Froth stabilisers decrease the wettability of gangue.
solution_text: Option (a) and (c). Collectors like sodium ethyl xanthate are added to enhance the non-wettability of sulphide ore particles. In the concentration of galena ore, sodium cyanide is used as a depressant for zinc sulphide. NaCN forms a complex with NaCN and deposits on the surface of ZnS which prevents it for the formation of Froth by selecting ore particle while PbS forms a froth. Thus, by using depressant, two sulphides ores can be separated by froth floatation method.
solution_html: Option (a) and (c). Collectors like sodium ethyl xanthate are added to enhance the non-wettability of sulphide ore particles. In the concentration of galena ore, sodium cyanide is used as a depressant for zinc sulphide. NaCN forms a complex with NaCN and deposits on the surface of ZnS which prevents it for the formation of Froth by selecting ore particle while PbS forms a froth. Thus, by using depressant, two sulphides ores can be separated by froth floatation method.

question_text: In the Froth Floatation process, zinc sulphide and lead sulphide can be separated by ________. using collectors. adjusting the proportion of oil to water. using depressant. using froth stabilisers.
question_html:
In the Froth Floatation process, zinc sulphide and lead sulphide can be separated by ________.

  1. using collectors.
  2. adjusting the proportion of oil to water.
  3. using depressant.
  4. using froth stabilisers.
solution_text: (b, c). The froth floatation method is used to extract metal from sulphide ore. ZnS and PbS can be separated by using depressants and adjusting the proportion of oil to water. The depressant used for this purpose is NaCN. It selectively prevents ZnS from coming to the froth and allows PbS to come with the froth.
solution_html: (b, c). The froth floatation method is used to extract metal from sulphide ore. ZnS and PbS can be separated by using depressants and adjusting the proportion of oil to water. The depressant used for this purpose is NaCN. It selectively prevents ZnS from coming to the froth and allows PbS to come with the froth.

question_text: Which of the following reactions occur during calcination? CaCO 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CaO + CO 2 2FeS 2 + {tex}\frac {11} {2}{/tex} O 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 2 O 3 + 4SO 2 Al 2 O 3 .x H 2 O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Al 2 O 3 + x H 2 O ZnS + {tex}\frac {3} {2}{/tex} O 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zno + SO 2
question_html:
Which of the following reactions occur during calcination?

  1. CaCO3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CaO + CO2
  2. 2FeS2 + {tex}\frac {11} {2}{/tex}O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe2O3 + 4SO2
  3. Al2O3 .x H2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Al2O3 + x H2O
  4. ZnS + {tex}\frac {3} {2}{/tex} O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zno + SO2
solution_text: (a, c) Calcination involves heating of the ore when the volatile matter escapes it leaves behind the metal oxide. Oxygen containing ores like oxide, hydroxides and carbonates are calcined. Thus, the following reactions occur during calcination. CaCO 3 {tex}\xrightarrow{\triangle }{/tex} CaO + CO 2 Al 2 O 3 . xH 2 O {tex}\xrightarrow{\triangle }{/tex} Al 2 O 3 + xH 2 O
solution_html:
(a, c) Calcination involves heating of the ore when the volatile matter escapes it leaves behind the metal oxide. Oxygen containing ores like oxide, hydroxides and carbonates are calcined. Thus, the following reactions occur during calcination.
CaCO3 {tex}\xrightarrow{\triangle }{/tex} CaO + CO2
Al2O3 . xH2O {tex}\xrightarrow{\triangle }{/tex} Al2O3 + xH2O

question_text: For the metallurgical process of which of the ores calcined ore can be reduced by carbon? haematite calamine iron pyrites sphalerite
question_html:
For the metallurgical process of which of the ores calcined ore can be reduced by carbon?

  1. haematite
  2. calamine
  3. iron pyrites
  4. sphalerite
solution_text: (a, b) Oxides can be reduced by using carbon. Haematite (Fe 2 O 3 .H 2 O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 2 O 3 +xH 2 O) and calamine (ZnCO 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ZnO + CO 2 ) can be reduced by carbon.
solution_html: (a, b) Oxides can be reduced by using carbon. Haematite (Fe2O3.H2O{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe2O3 +xH2O) and calamine (ZnCO3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ZnO + CO2) can be reduced by carbon.

question_text: Why is an external emf of more than 2.2V required for the extraction of Cl 2 from brine?
question_html: Why is an external emf of more than 2.2V required for the extraction of Cl2 from brine?
solution_text: For the following reaction of extraction of Cl 2 from brine solution: 2Cl - (aq) + 2H 2 O(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2OH - (aq) + H 2 (g) + Cl 2 (g) the value of Gibbs energy is {tex}\triangle G^{\ominus}{/tex} is +ve (422 kJ). For this value of (emf) E - cell is {tex}\triangle{/tex} G- = -nFE° or {tex}E^\ominus{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{n F}=-\frac{422 \times 10^{3} J}{2 \times 96500}{/tex} = -2.20 V Therefore, extraction of Cl 2 from brine solution will require an external emf of more than 2.20 V for the electrolysis process.
solution_html:
For the following reaction of extraction of Cl2 from brine solution:
2Cl-(aq) + 2H2O(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2OH-(aq) + H2(g) + Cl2(g) the value of Gibbs energy is {tex}\triangle G^{\ominus}{/tex} is +ve (422 kJ). For this value of (emf) E- cell is
{tex}\triangle{/tex}G- = -nFE°
or {tex}E^\ominus{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{n F}=-\frac{422 \times 10^{3} J}{2 \times 96500}{/tex} = -2.20 V
Therefore, extraction of Cl2 from brine solution will require an external emf of more than 2.20 V for the electrolysis process.

question_text: At temperatures above 1073K coke can be used to reduce FeO to Fe. How can you justify this reduction with Ellingham diagram?
question_html: At temperatures above 1073K coke can be used to reduce FeO to Fe. How can you justify this reduction with Ellingham diagram?
solution_text: As per Ellingham diagram(Gibbs energy vs temperature plots) at a temperature greater than 1073 K, {tex}\triangle G^\ominus{/tex} for the formation, FeO is less negative than {tex}\triangle G^\ominus{/tex} for the formation of carbon monoxide from carbon. Thus, above 1073 K, carbon can reduce FeO, i.e., {tex}\triangle_{r} G^\ominus{/tex} for the reaction. FeO + C {tex}\xrightarrow{{ > 1073}}{/tex} Fe + CO is negative.
solution_html:
As per Ellingham diagram(Gibbs energy vs temperature plots) at a temperature greater than 1073 K, {tex}\triangle G^\ominus{/tex} for the formation, FeO is less negative than {tex}\triangle G^\ominus{/tex} for the formation of carbon monoxide from carbon. Thus, above 1073 K, carbon can reduce FeO, i.e., {tex}\triangle_{r} G^\ominus{/tex} for the reaction.
FeO + C {tex}\xrightarrow{{ > 1073}}{/tex} Fe + CO is negative.

Limestone is added as flux and the impurities of sulphur, silicon and phosphorus change to their oxides and passes into slag.

question_text: How is copper extracted from low-grade copper ores?
question_html: How is copper extracted from low-grade copper ores?
solution_text: Copper is extracted by hydrometallurgy from low-grade ores. It is leached out using acid or bacteria (leaching processes in which ore is treated with a suitable reagent that dissolves ore but not the impurities). The solution containing Cu 2+ ions is treated with scrap iron or H 2 . The chemical reaction involved is as follows: Cu 2+ (aq) + H 2 (g) → Cu(s) + 2H + (aq).
solution_html: Copper is extracted by hydrometallurgy from low-grade ores. It is leached out using acid or bacteria (leaching processes in which ore is treated with a suitable reagent that dissolves ore but not the impurities). The solution containing Cu2+ ions is treated with scrap iron or H2. The chemical reaction involved is as follows: Cu2+(aq) + H2(g) → Cu(s) + 2H+ (aq).

question_text: Write two basic requirements for refining of a metal by Mond process and by Van Arkel Method.
question_html: Write two basic requirements for refining of a metal by Mond process and by Van Arkel Method.
solution_text: The two basic requirements for vapour phase refining metal by Mond's process for Ni and by van Arkel method for Zr and Ti are: The metal should form a volatile compound with an available reagent. The volatile compound should be easily decomposable so, that metal can be easily recovered.
solution_html:
The two basic requirements for vapour phase refining metal by Mond's process for Ni and by van Arkel method  for Zr and Ti are:

  1. The metal should form a volatile compound with an available reagent.
  2. The volatile compound should be easily decomposable so, that metal can be easily recovered.

question_html: How do we separate two sulphide ores by Froth Floatation Method? Explain with an example.
solution_text: The separation of two sulphide ores can be achieved by adjusting the proportion of oil to water or by using depressants. In the case of an ore containing ZnS and PbS, the depressant used is NaCN. It forms complex with ZnS and selectively prevents it from coming with froth but allows PbS to come with the froth. 4NaCN + ZnS {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na 2 Zn(CN) 4 + Na 2 S

question_text: The purest form of iron is prepared by oxidising impurities from cast iron in a reverberatory furnace. Which iron ore is used to line the furnace? Explain by giving a reaction.
question_html: The purest form of iron is prepared by oxidising impurities from cast iron in a reverberatory furnace. Which iron ore is used to line the furnace? Explain by giving a reaction.
solution_text: Haematite (Fe 2 O 3 ) is used it contains 4% of carbon and impurities in smaller amounts ( eg S, P, SI, Mn). It supplies the oxygen and oxidizes carbon, silicon, manganese, and phosphorus present in the cast iron to CO, SiO 2 , MnO and P 2 O 5 respectively. Fe 2 O 3 + 3C {tex}\xrightarrow{{{\text{Heat}}}}{/tex} 2Fe + 3CO 3Si + 2Fe 2 O 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4Fe + 3SiO 2 3S + 2Fe 2 O 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3SO 2 + 4Fe CO and SO 2 escape and MnO and SiO 2 combine to form manganous silicate (MnSiO 3 ) as slag. Mn + SiO 2 {tex}\xrightarrow{{{\text{Heat}}}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{MnSi}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{Maganous}}\,\,{\text{silicate}}\,{\text{(Slag)}}} {/tex}
solution_html:
Haematite (Fe2O3) is used it contains 4% of carbon and impurities in smaller amounts ( eg S, P, SI, Mn). It supplies the oxygen and oxidizes carbon, silicon, manganese, and phosphorus present in the cast iron to CO, SiO2, MnO and P2O5 respectively.
Fe2O3 + 3C {tex}\xrightarrow{{{\text{Heat}}}}{/tex} 2Fe + 3CO
3Si + 2Fe2O3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4Fe + 3SiO2
3S + 2Fe2O3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3SO2 + 4Fe
CO and SO2 escape and MnO and SiO2 combine to form manganous silicate (MnSiO3) as slag.
Mn + SiO2 {tex}\xrightarrow{{{\text{Heat}}}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{MnSi}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{Maganous}}\,\,{\text{silicate}}\,{\text{(Slag)}}} {/tex}

question_text: What is the role of flux in metallurgical processes?
question_html: What is the role of flux in metallurgical processes?
solution_text: The ores, even after concentration, contain some gangue. To remove gangue, certain substances are mixed with concentrated ore which combines with the gangue to form a fusible material that is not soluble in molten metal. The substances used are called fluxes. Acidic flux (are those flux that comes from a substance like a lewis acid) removes basic impurities while basic flux removes acidic impurities. {tex}\mathop {{\text{Si}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{{\text{Acidic}}\,{\text{acid}}} {\text{ + }}\mathop {{\text{CaO}}}\limits_{{\text{Basic}}\,{\text{impurity}}} {/tex} {tex}\to \mathop {{\text{CaSi}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{Slag}}} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{MgO}}}\limits_{{\text{Basic}}\,{\text{flux}}} + \mathop {{\text{Si}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{{\text{Acidic}}\,{\text{acid}}} {/tex} {tex} \to \mathop {{\text{MgSi}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{Slag}}} {/tex}
solution_html:
The ores, even after concentration, contain some gangue. To remove gangue, certain substances are mixed with concentrated ore which combines with the gangue to form a fusible material that is not soluble in molten metal. The substances used are called fluxes.
Acidic flux (are those flux that comes from a substance like a lewis acid) removes basic impurities while basic flux removes acidic impurities.
{tex}\mathop {{\text{Si}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{{\text{Acidic}}\,{\text{acid}}} {\text{ + }}\mathop {{\text{CaO}}}\limits_{{\text{Basic}}\,{\text{impurity}}} {/tex} {tex}\to \mathop {{\text{CaSi}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{Slag}}} {/tex}
{tex}\mathop {{\text{MgO}}}\limits_{{\text{Basic}}\,{\text{flux}}} + \mathop {{\text{Si}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{{\text{Acidic}}\,{\text{acid}}} {/tex}{tex} \to \mathop {{\text{MgSi}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{Slag}}} {/tex}

question_text: Write the chemical reactions involved in the extraction of gold by the cyanide process. Also, give the role of zinc in the extraction.
question_html: Write the chemical reactions involved in the extraction of gold by the cyanide process. Also, give the role of zinc in the extraction.
solution_text: Gold particles present in the ore are treated with a dilute solution of NaCN and air is circulated. Gold particles dissolve by forming a soluble complex. 4Au (s) + 8NaCN(aq) + 2H 2 O(aq) + O 2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{4Na[Au(CN}}{{\text{)}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{]}}}\limits_{{\text{Sodium}}\,{\text{dicyanoaurate(l)}}\,\,{\text{(soluble)}}\,} {/tex} (aq) + 4NaOH(aq) Au is recovered from the solution by the addition of electropositive metal like zinc. 2Na[Au(CN) 2 ] (aq) + Zn(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{[Zn(CN}}{{\text{)}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{]}}}\limits_{{\text{Sodium}}\,{\text{tetracyano}}\,{\text{zincate}}\,\,{\text{(ll)}}} {/tex} (aq) + 2Au(s) Extraction of gold by cyanide process involves both oxidation and reduction. Formation of cyanide complex involves oxidation of gold while its recovery involves reduction. Zn acts as a reducing agent.
solution_html:
Gold particles present in the ore are treated with a dilute solution of NaCN and air is circulated. Gold particles dissolve by forming a soluble complex.
4Au (s) + 8NaCN(aq) + 2H2O(aq) + O2 (g){tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{4Na[Au(CN}}{{\text{)}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{]}}}\limits_{{\text{Sodium}}\,{\text{dicyanoaurate(l)}}\,\,{\text{(soluble)}}\,} {/tex} (aq) + 4NaOH(aq)
Au is recovered from the solution by the addition of electropositive metal like zinc.
2Na[Au(CN)2] (aq) + Zn(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{[Zn(CN}}{{\text{)}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{]}}}\limits_{{\text{Sodium}}\,{\text{tetracyano}}\,{\text{zincate}}\,\,{\text{(ll)}}} {/tex}(aq) + 2Au(s)
Extraction of gold by cyanide process involves both oxidation and reduction. Formation of cyanide complex involves oxidation of gold while its recovery involves reduction. Zn acts as a reducing agent.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Elements
question_text: Explain the following : CO 2 is a better reducing agent below 710K whereas CO is a better reducing agent above 710K. Generally, sulphide ores are converted into oxides before reduction. Silica is added to the sulphide ore of copper in the reverberatory furnace. Carbon and hydrogen are not used as reducing agents at high temperatures. Vapour phase refining method is used for the purification of Ti.
question_html:
Explain the following :

  1. CO2 is a better reducing agent below 710K whereas CO is a better reducing agent above 710K.
  2. Generally, sulphide ores are converted into oxides before reduction.
  3. Silica is added to the sulphide ore of copper in the reverberatory furnace.
  4. Carbon and hydrogen are not used as reducing agents at high temperatures.
  5. Vapour phase refining method is used for the purification of Ti.
solution_text: According to the Ellingham diagram, at a temperature below 710 K, {tex}\triangle G^{\ominus}{/tex} {tex}_{{\text{(C}}{\text{.C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{)}}}{/tex} < {tex}\triangle G^{\ominus}{/tex} (C.CO) hence CO 2 is a better reducing agent. At temperature above 710 K, {tex}\triangle G^{\ominus}{/tex} {tex}_{{\text{(C}}{\text{.C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{)}}}{/tex} < {tex}\triangle G^{\ominus}{/tex} (C.CO) hence CO is a better reducing agent. Sulphide ores cannot be reduced easily but oxide ores can be easily reduced, hence sulphide ores are generally converted into oxides before reduction. Copper pyrites contain iron sulphide in addition to copper sulphide. In the reverberatory furnace, copper ore is roasted to give oxides. FeO is removed by adding silica from the matte containing Cu 2 S and FeS. 2FeS + 3O 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2FeO + 2SO 2 FeO + SiO 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{FeSi}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{(Slag)}}} {/tex} At the high temperature carbon and 'hydrogen react with metals to form carbides and hydrides respectively hence they are not used as reducing agents. Ti reacts with iodine to form Til 4 which is volatile and decomposes on a tungsten filament, electrically heated to about 1800K. The pure Ti is then deposited on the filament. {tex}\mathop {{\text{Ti}}}\limits_{{\text{Impure}}} {\text{ + 2}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{530K}}}}{\text{Ti}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{{\text{1800K}}}}\mathop {{\text{Ti}}}\limits_{{\text{Pure}}} {\text{ + 2}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. According to the Ellingham diagram, at a temperature below 710 K, {tex}\triangle G^{\ominus}{/tex}{tex}_{{\text{(C}}{\text{.C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{)}}}{/tex} < {tex}\triangle G^{\ominus}{/tex}(C.CO) hence CO2 is a better reducing agent. At temperature above 710 K, {tex}\triangle G^{\ominus}{/tex}{tex}_{{\text{(C}}{\text{.C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{)}}}{/tex} < {tex}\triangle G^{\ominus}{/tex}(C.CO) hence CO is a better reducing agent.
  2. Sulphide ores cannot be reduced easily but oxide ores can be easily reduced, hence sulphide ores are generally converted into oxides before reduction.
  3. Copper pyrites contain iron sulphide in addition to copper sulphide. In the reverberatory furnace, copper ore is roasted to give oxides. FeO is removed by adding silica from the matte containing Cu2S and FeS.
    2FeS + 3O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2FeO + 2SO2
    FeO + SiO2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{FeSi}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{(Slag)}}} {/tex}
  4. At the high temperature carbon and 'hydrogen react with metals to form carbides and hydrides respectively hence they are not used as reducing agents.
  5. Ti reacts with iodine to form Til4 which is volatile and decomposes on a tungsten filament, electrically heated to about 1800K. The pure Ti is then deposited on the filament.
    {tex}\mathop {{\text{Ti}}}\limits_{{\text{Impure}}} {\text{ + 2}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{530K}}}}{\text{Ti}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{{\text{1800K}}}}\mathop {{\text{Ti}}}\limits_{{\text{Pure}}} {\text{ + 2}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex}
